egest: verb. To eliminate undigested food or waste from the body (as feces).
eight: noun. NATO & ICAO radiotelephony clear code (spelling-alphabet name) for the digit 8.
eighty: numeral. The cardinal number occurring after seventy-nine and before eighty-one, represented in Roman numerals as LXXX and in Arabic
eth: noun. A letter (capital Ð, small ð) introduced into Old English to represent its dental fricative, then not distinguished from the letter
eye: noun. An organ through which animals see (“perceive surroundings via light”).
eyesight: noun. Vision or the faculty of sight.
gee: interjection. A general exclamation of surprise or frustration.
geest: noun. A type of slightly raised landscape, with sandy and gravelly soils, that occurs in the plains of Northern Germany, the Northern
gest: noun. A story or adventure; a verse or prose romance.
gets: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of get
ghee: noun. A type of clarified butter used in South Asian cooking.
gie: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Guébie.
gist: noun. The main idea or substance, or the most essential part, of a longer or more complicated matter; the crux, the heart, the pith.
git: noun. A silly, incompetent, stupid, or annoying person (usually a man).
heist: noun. A robbery or burglary, especially from an institution such as a bank or museum.
hey: interjection. An exclamation to get attention.
hie: verb. To hasten; to go quickly, to hurry.
hies: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of hie
hist: interjection. An utterance used to discreetly attract someone's attention.
see: verb. To perceive or detect someone or something with the eyes, or as if by sight.
set: verb. To put (something) down, to rest.
she: pronoun. The female (typically) person or animal previously mentioned or implied.
sheet: noun. A thin bed cloth used as a covering for a mattress or as a layer over the sleeper.
shit: noun. Usually solid excretory product evacuated from the bowels; feces.
sigh: verb. To inhale a larger quantity of air than usual, and immediately expel it; to make a deep single audible respiration, especially as the
sit: verb. To be in a position in which the upper body is upright and supported by the buttocks.
site: noun. The place where anything is fixed; situation; local position
sty: noun. A pen or enclosure for swine.
stye: noun. A bacterial infection in the eyelash or eyelid.
tee: noun. The name of the Latin script letter T / t.
teg: noun. A sheep (originally a ewe) in its second year, or from the time it is weaned until it is first shorn.
the: article. Used before a noun phrase, including a simple noun The definite grammatical article that shows that the noun phrase that
thee: pronoun. Objective and reflexive case of thou.
they: pronoun. A group of entities previously mentioned.
thy: determiner. Possessive form of thou: that which belongs to thee; which belongs to you.
tyee: noun. A tribal chief of the Chinookan peoples in the Pacific Northwest of the United States.
yeh: interjection. Alternative form of yeah; yes.
yes: particle. Used to show agreement or acceptance.
yet: adverb. Thus far; up to the present; up to some unspecified time.
yeti: noun. An unidentified humanoid animal said to live in the Himalayas.
gesith: noun. A companion to an athel or king in medieval England; a thegn; a comrade
geyest: adjective. superlative form of gey: most gey
gish: noun. A character that is skilled in both physical combat and the use of magic.
gye: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Gyem.
hyes: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of hye
ist: noun. A practitioner or supporter of an ism.
seity: noun. Something peculiar to oneself; personal peculiarity; individuality.
sey: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Secoya.
sheety: adjective. Resembling a sheet; wide and flat.
steg: verb. To conceal (data) by means of steganography.
stie: noun. Obsolete form of stye (“eye infection”).
thees: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of thee
thig: verb. To solicit or receive, usually by begging; ask as alms.
thigs: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of thig
tig: verb. To touch lightly or playfully; to trifle, dally.
